does: This paper introduces GOAT, an automated red teaming system that simulates adversarial conversations with large language models to identify safety vulnerabilities. Rather than crafting single optimized prompts, GOAT operates as an agentic system that engages in multi-turn dialogue with target LLMs, employing seven distinct adversarial prompting techniques instantiated through a general-purpose model. The system is designed to mimic how ordinary users interact with AI systems—using commonly shared techniques found online without requiring access to model internals or advanced adversarial ML knowledge. The approach generates conversational attacks that feel naturalistic while systematically exploring weaknesses in LLM safety training, enabling more representative assessment of real-world vulnerabilities than existing automated methods.
connects: This work responds to limitations in prior automated red teaming approaches that either require white-box access to model internals or rely on single-shot adversarial prompts unrealistic to actual user behavior. It builds on the growing literature in LLM safety and adversarial robustness assessment, extending beyond techniques like prompt injection and jailbreaking by operationalizing them within a multi-turn conversational framework. GOAT combines insights from agentic AI systems with adversarial prompting strategies, recognizing that modern LLM deployments operate in interactive settings. The research acknowledges that manual red teaming provides more realistic threat models but is prohibitively expensive, positioning automated but human-aligned methods as a middle ground between efficiency and ecological validity.
enables: This work enables more scalable and representative assessment of LLM safety across diverse adversarial scenarios without requiring human annotators or expensive manual red teaming campaigns. By implementing multiple attacks through agentic conversation, GOAT provides a reusable framework for systematically discovering failure modes that users might naturally encounter. The system's design lowers barriers to identifying vulnerabilities, potentially informing better defense mechanisms and safety fine-tuning approaches. Future work could extend the attack repertoire, incorporate feedback mechanisms to improve adversarial effectiveness, and apply similar conversational red teaming frameworks across different model architectures and deployment contexts, ultimately advancing understanding of real-world LLM safety.
